title: PGM-FI self-diagnostic blink codes — reading without a dealer tool
description: Honda PGM-FI equipped bikes (2001+) have a self-diagnostic system that blinks the FI warning light on the dash in a pattern that indicates the fault. No dealer tool required to read basic codes — just count the blinks. The FI light blinks long (tens digit) and short (ones digit) to give a 2-digit code.
fix_procedure: 1. To read codes: turn ignition ON (engine off). If FI light stays on, there's a stored code. 2. On some models, jump the diagnostic connector (2-pin under seat) to enter diagnostic mode. 3. Count blinks: long blinks = tens, short blinks = ones. Example: 1 long + 2 short = code 12 (injector circuit). 4. Common codes: 1=MAP sensor, 7=TPS, 8=intake air temp, 9=coolant temp, 11=cam position, 12=injector, 21=O2 sensor, 25=knock sensor, 33=fuel pump, 54=bank angle sensor. 5. To clear codes: fix the fault, then jump the diagnostic connector and turn ignition on/off 3 times. 6. Forum tip: the Honda FI blink code system is the most mechanic-friendly self-diagnostic on any Japanese bike — no expensive scan tool needed. Write down the code, look it up, fix it. The diagnostic connector is usually a 2-pin green or black connector tucked under the seat or behind a side cover. On newer models (2015+), the FI code also displays on the TFT or LCD dash directly as a number. Keep a cheat sheet of codes in your shop — the most common are 7 (TPS), 33 (fuel pump), and 54 (tip sensor after a drop).

title: HISS immobilizer system — all EFI Honda models
description: The Honda Ignition Security System (HISS) uses a transponder chip in the key that must be recognized by the ECU before the bike will fire. If the HISS indicator blinks rapidly and the bike cranks but won't start, the system isn't recognizing the key. Keys cannot be copied at a locksmith — only Honda dealers can program them.
causes: Key damage from impact or water
fix_procedure: 1. HISS indicator behavior: comes on briefly then off = system OK. Blinks rapidly and stays on = key not recognized. 2. Try the spare key — every Honda comes with 2 HISS keys. If spare works, primary key transponder is dead. 3. If no key works: check HISS antenna ring connector (around ignition barrel). Corrosion or loose connector is common. 4. If antenna and connectors are good: ECU HISS module has failed — dealer-only repair (Honda HDS tool required to pair keys). 5. Replacement HISS keys from Honda: $150-200 each, require dealer programming. 6. Forum tip: ALWAYS keep both HISS keys in separate locations — if you lose both, the dealer has to replace the ECU AND program new keys ($800+). The HISS antenna ring is a coil that reads the transponder — it fails from heat and vibration near the ignition. A replacement ring is $40-60 and takes 20 minutes. For track riders: some bypass the HISS system to avoid lockout during crashes — but this disables theft protection entirely. Never carry both keys on the same ring — if you lose the ring, you lose all keys.

title: Regulator/rectifier failure — the universal Honda problem
description: This is the definitive cross-model Honda reg/rec reference. Every Honda motorcycle from 1985-2020 with a shunt-type regulator is at risk. The shunt design converts excess AC to heat — it's literally designed to overheat. MOSFET replacement is the permanent fix across all Honda models.
causes: Shunt regulator designed to dissipate excess energy as heat
Poor OEM mounting in low-airflow areas
Stator connector melt from high current
fix_procedure: 1. TEST: engine at 5000 RPM, multimeter across battery — 13.5-14.5V. Below 13V = undercharging. Above 15V = overcharging (DANGEROUS — stop riding). 2. TEST STATOR: disconnect 3-pin yellow connector. AC voltage across each pair of leads at 5000 RPM — all 3 readings should be within 10% of each other. Each lead to ground = infinite (if not, stator is shorted to ground). 3. If stator is good and regulator output is wrong: REPLACE REGULATOR with MOSFET type. 4. ALWAYS hard-wire the stator connector — the plastic plug melts and is the #2 failure point. 5. Recommended MOSFET regulators by model: Rick's Motorsport (universal), Shindengen FH012AA (sport bikes), Electrosport ESR438 (VFR specific). 6. Forum tip: this is THE Honda reliability issue. If you work on Hondas, you will replace dozens of reg/recs per year. Stock the Rick's MOSFET universal regulator — it fits 90% of Hondas with minor connector adaptation. The stator connector hard-wire kit is $5 in parts (ring terminals, heat-shrink, solder) and prevents the #2 failure mode. Also, teach every Honda owner: if the headlight dims noticeably at idle, the charging system is on its way out — test it before it leaves them stranded.
parts_needed: Stator connector hard-wire kit (ring terminals, solder, heat-shrink)

title: Stator failure diagnosis and replacement — all Honda models
description: The Honda stator is the AC generator behind the engine cover. It fails from insulation breakdown caused by heat, oil contamination, and age. Always replace the regulator when replacing the stator — a failed regulator killed the stator, and will kill the new one too.
causes: Winding insulation breakdown from heat cycling
Oil contamination (some stators run in oil)
Stator connector melt creating resistance and heat
fix_procedure: 1. RESISTANCE TEST: disconnect stator 3-pin connector. Ohms across each pair of leads — should be 0.1-0.5 ohms (varies by model). Infinite = open winding. 2. GROUND TEST: each lead to engine ground — should be infinite. Any resistance = shorted to ground (stator is toast). 3. AC OUTPUT TEST: engine at 5000 RPM, AC volts across each pair. All 3 should be within 10%. If one is significantly lower, that winding is failing. 4. Replace stator: requires removing the left engine cover (most models). Drain any oil first. 5. ALWAYS replace stator AND regulator as a set. 6. Hard-wire the stator connector. 7. Forum tip: when you pull the engine cover to replace a stator, inspect the stator lead wires where they exit the engine case — the grommets deteriorate and the wires chafe on the case edge. Apply a new grommet or silicone sealant. Also, the stator bolts on some models (VFR, CBR) are Loctited from the factory — use a 6-point socket and controlled force. If a bolt strips, you're extracting it from the crankcase.

title: Starter relay and starter motor diagnosis
description: Honda starter circuits follow a consistent pattern across all models: battery → main fuse → ignition switch → kill switch → clutch/neutral safety switch → starter relay → starter motor. Understanding this chain helps diagnose any no-crank condition without guessing.
causes: Clutch switch not engaging (lever not fully pulled)
Side stand switch preventing start in gear
Battery voltage too low for relay to engage
fix_procedure: 1. NO CLICK when pressing start button: the relay isn't getting the signal. Check: kill switch position, clutch lever fully pulled (switch on perch), side stand UP if in gear, neutral light if in neutral. 2. CLICK but no crank: relay is engaging but can't pass enough current. Jump the two large terminals on the relay with a screwdriver (bike in neutral, stand clear). If starter spins, relay contacts are pitted — replace relay ($15-25). 3. SLOW CRANK: battery voltage too low. Check battery voltage under load (>10.5V while cranking). 4. GRINDING: starter clutch (sprag) is worn — starter motor spins but doesn't engage crankshaft. 5. Forum tip: 80% of Honda no-start calls are: dead battery (charge it), kill switch (flip it), side stand switch (stand up in gear), or clutch switch (pull lever fully). Check all four before going deeper. The starter relay is usually under the seat — it's a $15 part that takes 5 minutes to replace. Carry a spare. Also, the Honda clutch switch is on the clutch lever perch — it has a tiny plunger that wears. A common hack is to bypass it with a jumper, but this is a safety risk.

title: FI (Fuel Injection) light on — sensor and actuator faults
description: When the PGM-FI light stays on, the ECU has detected a fault in the fuel injection system. The bike usually still runs but may be in limp mode (reduced power, fixed timing). Common sensor faults: TPS, MAP, coolant temp, intake air temp, O2 sensor, bank angle sensor (tip sensor).
fix_procedure: 1. Read blink code (see Phase 33 issue #1 for procedure). 2. Code 1 (MAP sensor): check vacuum hose to MAP sensor for cracks. Test MAP sensor voltage. Replace if out of spec. 3. Code 7 (TPS): check TPS voltage — should be 0.45-0.55V at closed throttle. Adjust or replace. 4. Code 8 (intake air temp): check connector for corrosion. Sensor resistance should change with temperature. Replace if stuck. 5. Code 9 (coolant temp): same as code 8 but for coolant. Check connector and resistance. 6. Code 21 (O2 sensor): check for exhaust leaks first (leak gives false lean reading). Replace O2 sensor if confirmed failed. 7. Forum tip: the bank angle sensor (tip sensor, code 54) triggers after ANY drop or tip-over. It kills fuel and ignition to prevent fire. To reset: pick the bike up, turn ignition off, wait 5 seconds, turn back on. If the FI light stays on after resetting, the sensor itself is damaged from the impact — replace it ($30-50). This is the most common FI code after a parking lot tip-over.
parts_needed: Relevant sensor (TPS, MAP, coolant temp, air temp, O2, bank angle)

title: Ground connection corrosion — hidden electrical gremlin
description: Honda motorcycles have multiple ground points that corrode over time, causing intermittent electrical faults that mimic component failures. Dim headlights, flickering gauges, FI light coming on randomly, and ABS warnings can all trace back to a single corroded ground bolt.
causes: Paint or coating insulating ground contact points
fix_procedure: 1. Start at battery negative terminal: remove, clean to bright metal (wire brush), reinstall with dielectric grease. 2. Trace ground cable to frame: remove bolt, clean frame contact to bare metal, reinstall. 3. Find engine-to-frame ground strap (usually near front motor mount): clean both ends. 4. On EFI bikes: check ECU ground point (usually behind side cover or under seat). 5. Clean ALL ground points annually as preventive maintenance. 6. Forum tip: I keep a wire brush bit for my cordless drill just for cleaning motorcycle ground points. Hit every ground connection on the bike once a year — it takes 15 minutes and prevents 80% of 'mystery' electrical problems. The most overlooked ground on Hondas is the engine-to-frame strap — it's a braided wire between the engine case and the frame. If it's corroded, the entire engine becomes a floating ground and nothing electrical works properly. Also, add dielectric grease to every ground connection after cleaning — it prevents future corrosion without blocking electrical contact.

title: Headlight and turn signal bulb replacement — sealed beam vs H4
description: Older Hondas use sealed beam headlights that are expensive and dim. Modern Hondas use H4 or H7 replaceable bulbs. LED conversions improve visibility significantly but require compatible relay (turn signals) and adequate electrical capacity (headlight).
causes: Voltage spikes from failing regulator shortening bulb life
Corroded bulb socket increasing resistance and heat
fix_procedure: 1. Identify bulb type: sealed beam (one piece, older bikes) vs H4/H7 (replaceable, modern). 2. Replace with correct wattage — don't install higher wattage than stock without checking electrical capacity. 3. For LED conversion: headlight LED (H4 or H7) is a direct replacement on most bikes, but check current draw doesn't exceed stock. 4. For LED turn signals: install an LED-compatible flasher relay to prevent hyperflash. 5. Clean bulb sockets with contact cleaner. 6. Forum tip: LED headlight conversion is the single best visibility upgrade for any Honda — especially older models with weak sealed beam headlights. A $25 H4 LED bulb draws less power (15-25W vs 55W) and produces 2-3x more light. This frees up electrical capacity on bikes with weak charging systems (like the Rebel 250 or XR650L). For turn signals, the EP-27 adjustable LED relay works on all Hondas — $10 and universal. Also, don't handle halogen bulbs with bare fingers — the oil from your skin creates a hot spot that causes premature burnout.

title: Fuse diagnosis and fuse box corrosion
description: Honda fuse boxes (both blade-type and glass-tube on older models) develop corrosion that causes intermittent power loss to specific circuits. A fuse that 'tests good' with a multimeter can still fail under load if the fuse holder contacts are corroded. Fuse box location varies by model — usually under the seat or behind a side cover.
causes: Glass tube fuse end-cap corrosion (older models)
fix_procedure: 1. Locate fuse box (under seat or behind side cover). 2. Remove each fuse and inspect: look for corrosion on the fuse blade AND the fuse holder contacts. 3. Clean fuse holder contacts with fine sandpaper or a fuse contact cleaner tool. 4. Replace any blown fuses with correct amperage. 5. Apply dielectric grease to fuse contacts (prevents corrosion, doesn't block contact). 6. On older bikes with glass tube fuses: upgrade to a blade fuse box for more reliable connections. 7. Forum tip: the #1 diagnostic mistake with fuses is testing them in the holder with a test light. A corroded fuse holder can pass enough current for a test light but not enough for the actual load (headlight, fuel pump, etc.). Pull the fuse out, inspect the contacts visually, and clean both the fuse and the holder. Also, keep spare fuses in the toolbox under the seat — most Hondas have a spare fuse slot in the fuse box. A blown fuse is a SYMPTOM, not a cause — if a fuse keeps blowing, find the short before replacing the fuse again.
parts_needed: Blade fuse box upgrade (for glass-tube bikes)

title: Charging system preventive testing — annual check protocol
description: Honda charging systems should be tested annually as preventive maintenance. A failing system gives weeks of warning before total failure — dim headlight at idle, slow cranking, battery needing frequent charging. Catching it early prevents being stranded.
causes: Stator winding resistance increasing with age
fix_procedure: 1. BATTERY TEST (engine off): fully charged = 12.6-12.8V. Below 12.4V = not fully charged. Below 12.0V = sulfated/dead. 2. CHARGING TEST: engine at 5000 RPM, voltmeter across battery. Should read 13.5-14.5V. 3. LOAD TEST: headlight on high beam at 5000 RPM. Voltage should stay above 13.0V. If it drops below 13V with load, charging system is marginal. 4. STATOR AC OUTPUT: disconnect stator connector, measure AC voltage between each pair of leads at 5000 RPM. All 3 pairs should be within 10% of each other. 5. Log the readings annually — a gradual decline predicts failure. 6. Forum tip: the 5-minute annual charging test catches 90% of charging failures before they strand you. I do this on every Honda that comes through the shop for any reason — oil change, tire, whatever. Write the readings on a sticker inside the seat. If voltage at 5000 RPM dropped from 14.2V last year to 13.8V this year, the system is declining — plan the MOSFET upgrade now rather than waiting for a dead-battery tow. This is the highest-value preventive test on any Honda motorcycle.
